headline.value: Elevate your mental wellness with compassionate psychiatric care

description.value: We offer personalized psychiatric care and short-term psychotherapy to support your mental well-being and help improve your daily life. For your convenience, virtual consultations are also available.

alt.value: Calming moments of connection and wellness

heading.value: Compassionate care for a wide range of mental health needs

At Elevate, we individualize your psychiatric care and prioritize your privacy.
We use a HIPAA compliant telehealth platform to ensure your utmost confidentiality.

summary.value: Support for focus, impulsivity, and daily functioning with treatment plans designed for school, work, and home life.

value.value: ADHD affects how the brain works; it can make it hard to focus and stop yourself from acting on urges.
It can make it tough to pay attention and stay organized.
It can also affect how you feel and how you get along with others.
ADHD is not caused by a person's choices or behavior; it's a condition that starts in childhood.
ADHD is common; many adults have it, so you're not alone.
Having someone in your family with ADHD
Differences in how the brain looks or works
Exposure to smoking, alcohol, or drugs before birth
Being born too early (premature) or too small
Early exposure to lead
Growing up or living in a stressful home
Having other mental health conditions like anxiety or depression

note.value: Many things can work together to cause ADHD. Having these things doesn't mean you'll get ADHD for sure.

value.value: Getting distracted easily and forgetting things
Losing things or struggling to keep track of tasks
Feeling restless or having trouble sitting still
Interrupting others or making quick decisions
Starting things but not finishing them
Having trouble planning your day or meeting deadlines
Feeling frustrated or upset often

heading.value: How does it affect the brain?

description.value: In ADHD, these parts of the brain don't work well together. This can make it harder to focus, stay organized, control impulses, and manage emotions.
Helps you move smoothly and manage time
Controls movement and helps you stick to routines
Helps you focus, plan, and control impulses
Helps you switch between tasks and manage emotions
Helps you form routines and stay motivated

value.value: If ADHD is making it hard to keep a job, stay safe, or manage your mood
If staying organized or focused is consistently difficult
If work or relationships are being affected because of ADHD symptoms

urgentHeading.value: Get help right away if you:

value.value: If impulsive decisions are causing ongoing problems

description.value: Your health professional can help you choose the best treatment or combinations of treatments for you. Sometimes, people need to try a few different things before they find what works best.

value.value: Talk therapies
Medicines
Exercise as part of treatment

heading.value: Where can I get more help?

value.value: ADHD is a medical condition caused by differences in brain function, not laziness or lack of willpower.
Many adults were not diagnosed as children, but symptoms continue into adulthood.
Medicine, therapy, and healthy habits can help you focus and feel more in control.
Many adults have ADHD, and support is available to help manage it.

summary.value: Stabilizing mood shifts with close monitoring, clear communication, and a plan that fits your routine.

value.value: A brain condition that causes severe mood changes
People with bipolar disorder have very high ups (mania) and very low downs (depression)
These mood changes can last for days or weeks
Bipolar disorder can make it hard to work, take care of yourself, or do daily tasks
Health professionals can treat bipolar disorder with medicine and talk therapy
It can run in families (passed down from parents)
Changes in the brain play a role
Stressful or hard life events can trigger it
It may start after a big life change
Most people get it between ages 6 and 30
Other mental health problems may increase the chance of bipolar disorder

note.value: Health professionals are still learning about what causes bipolar disorder. It's usually not just one thing, but a mix of different reasons.

value.value: Feeling super happy and full of energy (or very grouchy) for days (mania)
Feeling very sad and tired for long periods (depression)
Not needing much sleep or sleeping too much
Thinking and talking very fast
Doing risky things without thinking first
Having severe mood swings that last for days or weeks

heading.value: How does it affect the brain?

description.value: In bipolar disorder, some of these areas might work differently than usual. This can affect how a person feels, thinks, or acts.

alt.value: Brain regions affected by bipolar disorder

description.value: Helps control your actions and emotions
Passes messages in the brain
Handles fear and worry
Helps with memory
Helps you move, feel good, and stay motivated
Controls sleep, stress, and other body functions

value.value: You have very high energy and can't sleep for days
You feel very sad and don't want to do anything
You have severe mood swings that make life hard
You do risky things you wouldn't usually do
You feel worried, angry, or scared for no reason
You have trouble at work, school, or with family
You feel like hurting yourself
You hear or see things that aren't real

urgentHeading.value: Get help right away if you:

value.value: Your thoughts don't make sense
You feel out of control or very confused

description.value: Your treatment may change depending on your mood: high (mania), low (depression), or steady (stable). Health professionals use these treatments together to help people with bipolar disorder feel better and live well.

value.value: Medicine
Talk therapy
Family education
Support groups
Brain treatments
Lifestyle changes

heading.value: Where can I get more help?

value.value: Bipolar disorder is a brain condition that causes severe mood changes. These changes can make daily life hard.
It is a lifelong condition, but medicine, therapy, and healthy habits can help you manage it.
You are not alone. Many people have bipolar disorder. Health professionals, family, and friends can help.

summary.value: Treatment for low mood, low energy, and loss of interest with a focus on helping you feel like yourself again.

value.value: A common mental health problem
Feeling very sad for a long time and not enjoying things you used to like
Lasts for at least 2 weeks and makes it hard to do everyday activities
It's more than just feeling down for a short time. It's a serious condition that affects how a person lives their life
Sad or stressful things happening in your life
Having family members who've had depression
Being someone who often feels sad or worries a lot
Having a big health problem that lasts a long time
Using drugs or drinking too much alcohol
Not having friends or family to help you

note.value: Depression usually happens because of a mix of these things, not just one. Everyone's experience is different.

value.value: Feeling very sad most of the time
Not liking things you used to enjoy
Eating or sleeping more or less than usual
Feeling very tired all the time
Finding it hard to think clearly
Feeling like you're not important
Thinking about dying

heading.value: How does it affect the brain?

description.value: In depression, some of these areas might work differently or look smaller than usual. This can affect how a person feels, thinks, or acts.
Helps control emotion
Passes messages in the brain
Handles fear and worry
Helps with memory
Helps you move, feel good, and stay motivated

value.value: You feel very sad for more than 2 weeks
You have trouble doing everyday things
You don't feel better on your own
You think about hurting yourself
You feel like life isn't worth living

urgentHeading.value: Get help right away if you:

value.value: Stay away from friends and family
Use drugs or alcohol to feel better

description.value: Your health professional can help you choose the best treatment or combinations of treatments for you. Sometimes, people need to try a few different things before they find what works best.

value.value: Talk therapy
Medicine
Lifestyle changes

value.value: Depression is a serious health problem
Depression is not your fault
Anyone can get depression

Health professionals can help treat depression

summary.value: Trauma-informed care for flashbacks, hypervigilance, and emotional distress after difficult life experiences.

value.value: PTSD means past trauma still affects you. It can happen after scary or dangerous events, like accidents, war, or abuse.
It can make you feel upset or afraid even when you're safe. Loud noises, certain places, or memories can bring back bad feelings.
You may have trouble sleeping or feel on edge. Nightmares, bad dreams, or feeling jumpy are common.
PTSD can cause strong emotions. You might feel sad, angry, or alone, and sometimes it's hard to enjoy things you used to like.
Going through or seeing something very scary or dangerous (trauma)
Seeing someone else get hurt or killed (trauma)
Having a history of trauma or stress
Not having support after a trauma
Having anxiety, depression, or other mental health issues
Using drugs or alcohol to cope

note.value: Not everyone who goes through a trauma gets PTSD. Our brains and bodies deal with stress in different ways. Having support and good ways to cope can help protect you from PTSD.

value.value: You may keep thinking about the scary event or have bad dreams
Feeling scared or nervous all the time
Avoiding people or places that remind you of the event
Feeling angry, sad, or numb
Trouble sleeping or focusing
You may blame yourself for what happened, even if it wasn't your fault

heading.value: How does it affect the brain?

description.value: In PTSD, some of these areas might work differently or look smaller than usual. This can affect how a person feels, thinks, or acts.
Helps you make choices and control your feelings
Sorts information from your senses
Spots danger and creates feelings
Stores memories and links them to feelings
Manages breathing, heartbeat, and sleep

value.value: You have nightmares or scary thoughts about a past trauma
You feel more angry, scared, or sad than usual
You are avoiding things that remind you of a past trauma
You have trouble sleeping or focusing
You feel alone or don't want to be with others
Have thoughts of hurting yourself or others

urgentHeading.value: Get help right away if you:

value.value: Feel so upset you can't do daily tasks
Use drugs or alcohol to cope with your feelings

description.value: Your health professional can help you choose the best treatment or combinations of treatments. Sometimes, people need to try different treatments before they find what works best.

value.value: Trauma-focused talk therapies
Medicines
Mindfulness or relaxation practice
Support groups

heading.value: Where can I get more help?

value.value: PTSD can happen after very scary events or trauma
It causes nightmares and scary feelings
You might avoid things that remind you of what happened
It's not your fault
Health professionals can help you feel better

summary.value: Careful evaluation and ongoing support for symptoms that affect perception, thinking, and daily stability.

value.value: A serious brain problem that changes how people think and act
People with schizophrenia might see or hear things that aren't really there
They might think things are true even when others tell them it's not real
It can make it hard for people to do everyday things like work or take care of themselves
It often runs in families, so you might get it if your parents or siblings have it
Problems during pregnancy or birth, like being born too early or at a low weight
Very stressful life events, like losing a loved one or being abused as a child
Using drugs like marijuana, especially when you're young
Living in a city
Having a hard time in school or with making friends

note.value: Remember, having these things doesn't mean you'll definitely get schizophrenia. Health professionals are still learning about what causes it.

value.value: Seeing or hearing things that aren't really there, like voices or people
Believing things that aren't true, even when others say they're not real
Having trouble thinking clearly or speaking in a way that makes sense
Feeling less interested in doing things and showing less emotion
Moving in unusual ways, like being very still or moving a lot for no reason

heading.value: How does it affect the brain?

description.value: In schizophrenia, some of these areas might work or look smaller than usual. This can affect how a person feels, thinks, or acts.
Controls how we think and behave
Helps control how we move and think
Passes information in the brain
Handles emotion and fear
Controls memory and learning

value.value: You think about hurting yourself or others
You act very strange all of a sudden
You can't eat or clean yourself
You feel strange for more than 2 weeks
You have trouble doing normal things
You see or hear things others don't
You believe things that aren't true

urgentHeading.value: Get help right away if you:

value.value: You act very different suddenly
You stay away from friends and family
You can't think or talk clearly

description.value: Health professionals often use these treatments together to help people with schizophrenia feel better and live their lives.

value.value: Medicine
Talk therapy
Family support
Support groups
Brain treatments
Learning new skills

heading.value: Where can I get more help?

value.value: Schizophrenia is a serious health problem
Schizophrenia usually starts when people are young adults
Health professionals can help treat it with medicine and other treatments
With treatment, many people with schizophrenia can live good lives

Rohzan is a Board-Certified Psychiatric Nurse Practitioner specializing in ages across the life span. She is licensed to practice in the state of California and Nevada. She specializes in ADHD, autism related behavioral problems, Anxiety Disorder, Depression, Bipolar Disorder, and PTSD.

Rohzan utilizes her extensive experience as a psychiatric nurse to provide individualized care to every patient. She understands that mental health care requires careful examination of all aspects in every patient's life to support healing and stability.
